name: Spanish Language
description: Castellano, the eñe, and the Romance language with 500+ million native speakers across 20 countries.

questions.q: Approximately how many native Spanish speakers are there worldwide?

source.notes: Roughly 484-500 million native speakers; ~558 million total speakers including L2.

questions.q: Spanish is the second-most spoken native language in the world. What's #1?

source.notes: Over 900 million native Mandarin speakers.

questions.q: Which language family does Spanish belong to?

source.notes: Specifically the Ibero-Romance branch of Romance languages — descended from Vulgar Latin.

questions.q: Approximately how many countries have Spanish as an official language?

source.notes: Spain plus 19 in the Americas, Africa (Equatorial Guinea), and elsewhere.

questions.q: Which country has the largest population of native Spanish speakers?

source.notes: Around 130 million Spanish speakers in Mexico — more than any other country.

questions.q: Spanish is one of the six official languages of which international body?

source.notes: The six are: Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, Spanish.

questions.q: Which letter is unique to the Spanish alphabet (vs. English)?

source.notes: The 'eñe' represents the palatal nasal /ɲ/ sound.

questions.q: How many vowel phonemes does Spanish have?

source.notes: Same as the five Latin script vowels.

questions.q: By what other name is the Spanish language commonly known, especially in Spain?

source.notes: Originated as the language of the Kingdom of Castile.

questions.q: Which Spanish author has the language nicknamed 'la lengua de ___' after him?

source.notes: Cervantes wrote 'Don Quixote', often called the first modern novel.

questions.q: Who wrote 'Gramática de la lengua castellana' (1492), the first modern European grammar?

source.notes: Famously told Queen Isabella that 'language is the instrument of empire.'

questions.q: Spanish primarily evolved from which form of Latin?

source.notes: The everyday spoken Latin of the Roman Empire's common people.

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of modern Spanish vocabulary is of Latin origin?

source.notes: About 75% Latin, ~8% Arabic, plus Greek, Germanic, indigenous American, and English borrowings.

questions.q: Approximately how many Spanish words are derived from Arabic, due to centuries of Moorish rule in Iberia?

source.notes: Words like 'azúcar' (sugar), 'ojalá' (hopefully), 'almohada' (pillow).

questions.q: What is the body that regulates the Spanish language as a recognized standard?

source.notes: Founded in Madrid in 1713; works with the Asociación de Academias to standardize across all Spanish-speaking countries.

questions.q: What is the term for the Spanish phonological merger of 'll' and 'y' (so 'pollo' and 'poyo' sound alike)?

source.notes: Now standard in most of the Spanish-speaking world.

questions.q: What is the term for the Castilian Spanish distinction between /θ/ (as in 'cinco') and /s/ ('siete')?

source.notes: Most of Latin America uses 'seseo' — pronouncing both letters as /s/.

questions.q: What is the use of 'vos' instead of 'tú' for the second-person singular, common in Argentina and Uruguay?

source.notes: Voseo. Particularly characteristic of Argentine Spanish.

questions.q: Which non-Latin alphabet was Spanish's predecessor language Iberian once written in?

source.notes: Pre-Roman Iberian languages had their own scripts; Spanish itself has been written in Latin script throughout its history.

questions.q: Which Spanish-speaking country in Africa has Spanish as one of its official languages?

source.notes: Equatorial Guinea — a former Spanish colony — is the only African country with Spanish as an official language.
